steps.prompt: Which is the main finite verb?

hints.conversus: 'Conversus' is a perfect passive participle meaning 'having turned'
hints.Dominus: 'Dominus' is a noun meaning 'the Lord' — the subject
hints.Petrum: 'Petrum' is a proper noun in the accusative — the direct object

steps.target: to look back at / to regard
choices: to look back at / to regard

hints.to respect: 'To respect' is a later meaning of 'respicere' — here it means 'to look back at'

hints.present indicative: Present would be 'respicit' — 'respexit' with -exit is perfect
hints.future perfect: Future perfect would be 'respexerit' — 'respexit' is perfect

hints.aspicio, aspicere, aspexi, aspectum: These are the parts of 'aspicio' (to look at) — 'respicere' has the prefix re- (back)
hints.specto, spectare, spectavi, spectatum: These are the parts of 'specto' (to watch) — 'respicere' is re- + specere
hints.suspicio, suspicere, suspexi, suspectum: These are the parts of 'suspicio' (to look up at / to suspect)

steps.reveal: conversus = having turned (perf. pass. part., used as a circumstantial participle)

hints.present active participle: Present active participle would be 'convertens' — 'conversus' is perfect passive
hints.gerundive: Gerundive would be 'convertendus' — 'conversus' is a perfect passive participle
hints.finite verb: 'Conversus' is a participle, not a finite verb form

hints.verto, vertere, verti, versum: These are the parts of 'verto' (to turn) — 'convertere' is com- + vertere
hints.converso, conversare, conversavi, conver: These would be the parts of 'conversare' (to live with) — different verb from 'convertere'
hints.confero, conferre, contuli, collatum: These are the parts of 'confero' (to bring together/compare)

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'domini' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -us — 'domini' ends in -i

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'Petrus' — 'Petrum' with -um is accusative

steps.prompt: What temporal relationship does 'conversus' express relative to 'respexit'?
steps.target: prior action: 'having turned' (first), then 'he looked at' (second)
choices: prior action: 'having turned' (first), then 'he looked at' (second)
steps.reveal: Perfect participles typically express action prior to the main verb

hints.simultaneous action: Present participles express simultaneous action — perfect participles express prior action
hints.subsequent action: Future participles express subsequent action — perfect participles express prior action
hints.no temporal relationship: Participles inherently carry temporal information relative to the main verb

translation: And the Lord turned and looked at Peter
